seoTitle: Calculate Project Percent Complete in Smartsheet
metaDescription: Roll up task progress into a single project completion percentage in Smartsheet using parent-child hierarchies, weighted formulas, and Sheet Summary.
intro: Manually tracking overall project completion is error-prone once you have dozens of subtasks. This guide covers structuring percent columns, using parent-child hierarchies with rollup formulas, and displaying a single master completion percentage in the Sheet Summary panel.

steps.heading: Format your percent columns
steps.body: Ensure the columns you're tracking are properly formatted as percent data types.

steps.heading: Build parent-child hierarchies
steps.body: Establish project phases and their corresponding subtask rows using indentation.

steps.heading: Write rollup formulas
steps.body: Use functions like CHILDREN and AVGW to automatically summarize task progress into parent rows.

steps.heading: Use the Dependencies Engine for duration-based rollups
steps.body: Smartsheet can calculate progress natively through automated Duration column mappings in the Dependencies Engine settings.

steps.heading: Display a master completion card
steps.body: Use the Sheet Summary panel to isolate and show a single project-level completion percentage.

faq.question: How do I roll subtask progress up to a project-level percentage?
faq.answer: Use rollup formulas like CHILDREN and AVGW on parent rows, or enable the Dependencies Engine's automated duration-based calculation.

faq.question: Can I weight some tasks more heavily than others?
faq.answer: Yes, weighted rollup formulas such as AVGW let you factor task weight into the overall completion calculation.

faq.question: Where can I see the overall project completion number?
faq.answer: The Sheet Summary panel can display a single master card showing your project-level completion percentage.

faq.question: What happens if some cells are blank?
faq.answer: Blank cells and zero-day milestones can cause formula errors, so it's worth troubleshooting and handling them explicitly in your rollup formulas.
